bio.bio_blurb: Author of classical retellings and biographies for youth

career.organization: Schools in Germany, England, and the U.S.

narrative_blocks.header: She made the ancient world speak to children
narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of the British historian Sir Robert Ensor, Olivia Coolidge taught classics before publishing twenty-seven books, beginning with "Greek Myths" in 1949. Her retellings brought Homer and the Greek legends to generations of young readers.

narrative_blocks.header: A Newbery honor and a shelf of lives
narrative_blocks.body: Her "Men of Athens" was a 1963 Newbery Honor book, and she wrote young-adult biographies of Churchill, Gandhi, Edith Wharton, Eugene O’Neill, and Tom Paine. Critics praised her objective scholarship that credited genius without hiding its flaws.

narrative_blocks.header: She married into a Harvard dynasty at thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: In 1946 the London-born classicist married Archibald Cary Coolidge of Connecticut, a widower with four children, and became a naturalized American. She wrote on into her nineties, living to ninety-eight.

narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of the British historian Sir Robert Ensor, Olivia Coolidge taught classics before publishing twenty-seven books, beginning with "Greek Myths" in 1949. Her retellings brought Homer and the Greek legends to generations of young readers.

narrative_blocks.header: A Newbery honor and a shelf of lives
narrative_blocks.body: Her "Men of Athens" was a 1963 Newbery Honor book, and she wrote young-adult biographies of Churchill, Gandhi, Edith Wharton, Eugene O’Neill, and Tom Paine. Critics praised her objective scholarship that credited genius without hiding its flaws.

narrative_blocks.header: She married into a Harvard dynasty at thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: In 1946 the London-born classicist married Archibald Cary Coolidge of Connecticut, a widower with four children, and became a naturalized American. She wrote on into her nineties, living to ninety-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: He shared a name with a famous uncle
narrative_blocks.body: A Hotchkiss schoolmaster for twenty years and a Navy officer in the Second World War, Archibald Cary Coolidge lived to ninety-nine. His second wife was the children’s author Olivia Coolidge, and he carried the name of his great-uncle, Harvard’s first library director.
